coredata.dc:title: Improved photoelectrochemical performance of TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)@CDs@AgNPs and application for the detection of ultralow level AβO
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Jianwei
Lin, Danni
Wang, Shaopeng
Liao, Qihong
Meng, Fanhui
Chen, Jinghua
Han, Zhizhong
coredata.dc:description: TiO2 was successfully synthesized within the internal pores of MIL-101(Cr), leading to the formation of a TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr) composite. This heterojunction structure significantly improved the photogenerated electron-hole separation within the metal-organic framework (MOF) composite. Subsequently, carbon dots (CDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were introduced to further modify the TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr), resulting in a multimodified MIL-101(Cr) composite. The integration of CDs enhanced the light absorption capabilities of the composite, thereby boosting the photocurrent. Meanwhile, AgNPs not only enhanced the absorption of visible light through the local surface plasmon resonance effect (LSPR) but also facilitated efficient electron transport. This multimodal modification strategy led to a remarkable enhancement in the photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance of MIL-101(Cr), with the photocurrent density (J) increasing by approximately a factor of 19. To further expand the functionality of this composite, AβO capture DNA (cDNA) and CuS-binding aptamer were grafted onto the TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)@CDs@AgNPs, creating a sandwich-like structure. Based on this multimodified MIL-101(Cr) composite, an "on-off-on" type PEC biosensor was constructed. The p-type semiconductor CuS served as a signal amplifier, capturing photogenerated electrons and effectively reducing the photocurrent. When cDNA hybridized with AβO, the Apt-CuS moiety detached from the MIL-101(Cr) composite, leading to the restoration of the photocurrent. The PEC biosensor for the detection of AβO exhibited optimal performance at a pH of 7.00, an incubation temperature of 37 °C, and an incubation time of 45 min. Under these conditions, the biosensor demonstrated a wide detection range of 5 fM to 1 μM, with an ultralow detection limit of 4.36 fM. This method exhibits high sensitivity, robust anti-interference capabilities, and holds great promise for applications in tracing the detection of AβO in human serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MIL-101(Cr)
MOFs
Photoelectrochemical performance
AβO

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of TMCA and detection of AβO
Construction and condition optimization of the PEC biosensor

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2019J01300 and 2023J011224), Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates (202210392003).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Optimization of AgNPs content in TMCA, (B) TEM and (C) HRTEM image of TMCA, (D) XRD patterns of MIL-101(Cr), TMCA and simulated, (E) UV–visible DRS and (F) photocurrent response of the MOFs composites. Simulated XRD pattern is based on the cif-file of MIL-101(Cr) (CCDC No. 605510) [40].

(A) EIS spectra and (B) photocurrent response of the PEC biosensor during the construction process (a–e: TMCA, TMCA + cDNA, TMCA + cDNA + BSA, TMCA + cDNA + BSA + Apt-CuS and TMCA + cDNA + BSA + Apt-CuS + AβO (0.1 μM)), (C) the photocurrent response of the working electrodes without CuS (a–b: TMCA-cDNA + BSA and TMCA-cDNA + BSA + Apt, c–g: TMCA-cDNA + BSA + Apt + AβO (100 pM, 1 nM, 10 nM, 0.1 μM, 0.5 μM)).

Optimization of conditions, (A) incubation time of cDNA, (B) incubation temperature of cDNA, (C) concentration of cDNA, (D) concentration of Apt, (E) pH, (F) incubation time and (G) incubation temperature of AβO.

(A) J of the PEC biosensor with different concentrations of AβO ((a) 5 fM, (b) 10 fM, (c) 100 fM, (d) 1 pM, (e) 10 pM, (f) 100 pM, (g) 1 nM, (h) 10 nM, (i) 100 nM, (j) 0.5 μM, (k) 1 μM and (l) 1.5 μM), (B) linear regression curves, (C) selective investigation, (D) storage stability and (E) reproducibility.
(A) The TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)@CDs@AgNPs preparation process, (B) the working principle diagram of the PEC biosensor, and (C) the transfer process of the photogenerated electrons.
Detection of AβO in human serum.

head.ce:title: Improved photoelectrochemical performance of TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)@CDs@AgNPs and application for the detection of ultralow level AβO

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, 350122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, 350122, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2 was successfully synthesized within the internal pores of MIL-101(Cr), leading to the formation of a TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr) composite. This heterojunction structure significantly improved the photogenerated electron-hole separation within the metal-organic framework (MOF) composite. Subsequently, carbon dots (CDs) and silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were introduced to further modify the TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr), resulting in a multimodified MIL-101(Cr) composite. The integration of CDs enhanced the light absorption capabilities of the composite, thereby boosting the photocurrent. Meanwhile, AgNPs not only enhanced the absorption of visible light through the local surface plasmon resonance effect (LSPR) but also facilitated efficient electron transport. This multimodal modification strategy led to a remarkable enhancement in the photoelectrochemical (PEC) performance of MIL-101(Cr), with the photocurrent density (J) increasing by approximately a factor of 19. To further expand the functionality of this composite, AβO capture DNA (cDNA) and CuS-binding aptamer were grafted onto the TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)@CDs@AgNPs, creating a sandwich-like structure. Based on this multimodified MIL-101(Cr) composite, an "on-off-on" type PEC biosensor was constructed. The p-type semiconductor CuS served as a signal amplifier, capturing photogenerated electrons and effectively reducing the photocurrent. When cDNA hybridized with AβO, the Apt-CuS moiety detached from the MIL-101(Cr) composite, leading to the restoration of the photocurrent. The PEC biosensor for the detection of AβO exhibited optimal performance at a pH of 7.00, an incubation temperature of 37 °C, and an incubation time of 45 min. Under these conditions, the biosensor demonstrated a wide detection range of 5 fM to 1 μM, with an ultralow detection limit of 4.36 fM. This method exhibits high sensitivity, robust anti-interference capabilities, and holds great promise for applications in tracing the detection of AβO in human serum.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water-soluble Aβ oligomers (AβO) can bind to brain cell membrane receptors through strong synaptic action and destroy brain cell membrane function. Thus, AβO has strong neurotoxicity, leads to neuron death and early symptoms of alzheimer's disease (AD) [1,2]. Moreover, the production and accumulation of AβO in AD patients is much earlier than other theoretical pathogenic physiological processes, such as the formation of amyloid plaques and hyperphosphorylation of tau proteins. Therefore, highly neurotoxic AβO is considered an important marker in the diagnosis of AD [3]. The routine detection method mainly focuses on the analysis of cerebrospinal fluid, and the sampling process requires lumbar puncture, which is easily causing harm to the human body. On the contrary, blood contains abundant resources of proteome species and is relatively easy to collect and analyze, so the analysis of AβO in blood (serum and plasma) is more simple and reliable [4]. However, AβO content in blood is much smaller than that of cerebrospinal fluid. Therefore, there is an urgent need to explore and develop novel efficient, sensitive and reliable strategies and feasible minimally invasive detection methods to trace AβO in blood.

Currently, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, fluorescence detection, electrochemistry, and electrochemiluminescence detection techniques have made some progress in the detection of AβO, but there are some defects such as limited sensitivity, complicated operation, and long detection time [5–8]. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) analysis is an analytical method developed after optical and electrochemical analysis, which has great potential for application. PEC analysis has the advantages of low background noise detection, high sensitivity, wide dynamic range, and easy operation [9]. Compared to the optical and electrochemical methods, the excitation light source used in PEC is separated from the detection signal, which can avoid the interference of the background signal and thus has higher sensitivity [10,11]. Therefore, PEC is an ideal method for the detection of biomolecules.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a kind of crystalline porous material with periodic network structure, which is composed of inorganic metal centers (metal ions or metal clusters) and organic ligands assembled by coordination bonds [12]. MIL-101(Cr) is a representative MOFs composed of Cr ions and terephthalic acid ligands [13–17]. It has strong hydrothermal stability, large specific surface area and pore size, and the potential to generate unsaturated Cr sites, but MIL-101(Cr) has weak PEC performance, which limits its application in the field of PEC detection. At present, the TiO2-MIL-101(Cr) composites have garnered significant attention in recent reports, primarily attributed to the remarkable adsorption capabilities exhibited by MIL-101(Cr) and the exceptional photocatalytic properties of TiO2 [18–22]. Furthermore, the integration of MIL-101(Cr) with TiO2 results in a composite that demonstrates superior photocatalytic efficiency compared to its individual components. This enhanced performance can be attributed to the effective charge separation within the TiO2-MIL-101(Cr) composite, which facilitates the transfer of photogenerated electrons and holes, thereby promoting photocatalytic reactions. In our previous work, TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) were also used to modify MIL-101(Cr) to improve photocatalytic efficiency [23]. The results indicated that the TiO2-MIL-101(Cr) composite not only enhanced the photocatalytic performance, but also increased the photocurrent. Interestingly, the different TiO2 binding sites on MIL-101(Cr) exhibited distinct PEC properties. As Fig. S1 shown, the TiO2 NPs formed in the pores of MIL-101(Cr) (TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)) had better PEC performance than that of TiO2 formed on the surfaces of MIL-101(Cr) (TiO2-on-MIL-101(Cr)). However, the photocurrent was still lower than 0.40 μA for TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr). This may be due to the broad bandgap of TiO2, which inhibits the adsorption of visible light. Therefore, it is necessary to further modify the TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr) composites.

Carbon dots (CDs) have excellent biocompatibility and optical properties [24–27]. Modification of CDs onto the MOFs composite can promote light absorption and improve current transmission capacity. Moreover, the surface of CDs contains abundant oxygen-containing groups, which are beneficial for binding biological molecules. For PEC sensors, the charge transfer rate is an important factor. Silver has a higher electric conductivity among all metals and can enhance the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [28,29]. Additionally, AgNPs have local surface plasmon resonance effect (LSPR), which can improve the absorption of visible light from the MOFs composite.

Herein, we synthesized CDs in situ on the surface of TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr) and further modified AgNPs on the composite. The modification of CDs and AgNPs efficiently accelerated the electron transfer, enhanced the PEC properties, and improved the capture of biomolecules by MOFs composite. Additionally, p-type semiconductor CuS could capture the photogenerated electron and regulate the photocurrent. And then, CuS was bound with AβO aptamer (Apt). The Apt-CuS, capture DNA (cDNA), and CDs and AgNPs comodified MOFs (TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)@CDs@AgNPs) were fabricated as a sandwich structure. Through further signal amplification with CuS, an enhanced PEC biosensor based on the sandwich structure was developed for the sensitive detection of AβO. The developed biosensor displayed high sensitivity, selectivity, and reliability.
Reagents and instruments were shown in the Supplemental Material.
The syntheses of MIL-101(Cr) and TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr) composites were shown in the Supplemental Material.

The TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)@CDs preparation process was as follows. 0.480 g of citric acid, 0.100 g of TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr), 0.100 g of polyvinyl polyamine and 0.170 g of AlCl3 were dispersed in 35 mL of ultrapure water, sonicated and stirred for 15 min. The reaction solution was transferred to a stainless steel reactor lined with 50 mL of polytetrafluoroethylene and reacted at 180 °C for 5 h. The product was washed three times in deionized water and then vacuum dried at 60 °C for 8 h.

TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)@CDs@AgNPs was prepared by a simple dipping method. AgNPs reserve solution was prepared as follows [30]. 0.100 g of AgNO3 was added to 65 mL of ultrapure water, followed by 10 mL of polyethylpyrrolidone aqueous solution (PVP, 1.1 w/v %). The pH was adjusted to 10.00 with NH3∙H2O (2.8 %). Upon stirring, the solution was heated to 80 °C and 5 mL of H2O2 (0.15 %) was added drop by drop. After reaction for 5 min, the solution was centrifuged. The captured nanoparticles were washed with deionized water three times and dried at 60 °C for 8 h. The entire preparation process should avoid light operation. Finally, 10 mg mL−1 of the aqueous solution of TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)@CDs was mixed with 0.5 mg mL−1 of the colloidal AgNPs solution in a certain volume ratio. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 3 min and then stirred for 30 min TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)@CDs@AgNPs composite (TMCA) was obtained.

CuS NPs were synthesized using an improved method [31]. 15 μL of MPA was added to 50 mL of 2.0 mM Cu(NO3)2 aqueous solution and the pH was adjusted to 9.00 using a 0.5 M NaOH aqueous solution. Nitrogen was injected into the above solution for more than 30 min and then 5 mM of Na2S aqueous solution was added drop by drop until the molar ratio of Cu(NO3)2 to Na2S was 2.5. The dark green product was collected after a 24 h reaction. CuS NPs with carboxyl groups were obtained by washing the green products three times with ethanol and deionized water.

The AβO aptamer (Apt) bound CuS nanoparticles through an amide bond reaction. First, 100 μL EDC solution (20 mg mL−1) and 100 μL NHS solution (10 mg mL−1) were added to 1 mL of CuS solution (0.5 mg mL−1), incubated at room temperature for 30 min and then centrifuged to remove supernatant. Next, 1 mL of amino-labeled Apt (dissolved in TE buffer) was added and gently shaken at 4 °C for 12 h, and then centrifuged at 4 °C for 30 min (1000 rpm). The resulting precipitation was washed three times with Tris-HCl (10 mM, C NaCl = 0.1 M, pH 7.40) and re-dispersed in 1 mL of Tris-HCl before being stored at 4 °C for later use.

AβO was prepared according to previous reports [32,33]. HFIP was used to dissolve the Aβ lyophilized powder at a concentration of 1 mg mL−1. Then, the solution was sonicated for 10 min to monomerize the preexisting aggregates. After HFIP was removed with a mild nitrogen flow, the product (AβM) was reconstituted in 20 mM NaOH to a concentration of 2 mM. The sample was diluted to the desired concentration using 10 mM of PBS buffer at pH 7.40. The AβO and Aβ fibrils (AβF) were acquired through incubation of AβM (2 mM PBS, pH 7.40) in the dark at 37 °C for 24 h and 72 h, respectively. The concentration of the solution was calculated according to Lamberbier's law (A = abc), where the molar absorption coefficient a was 1410 L mol−1 cm−1, and the optical path b was 1 cm. Therefore, the molar concentration of the product (AβM, AβF and AβO) was calculated by measuring the absorbance A of the samples at 276 nm.

The ITO substrates were cleaned stepwise in detergent, acetone, ethanol, and deionized water with sonication and dried in an oven. Clean ITO electrodes were stored in anhydrous ethanol. The Mott-Schottky experiment, photocurrent response, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out at room temperature utilizing an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, CH Instruments) with a three-electrode mode: ITO loaded with the as-prepared nanocomposites was used as the working electrode, with Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and platinum wire as the counter electrode. The working electrode was prepared using a drip coating method. Specifically, a 60 μL composite mixture was dropped onto the ITO substrate, which was dried at 37 °C for 1 h. The temperature was then raised to 60 °C and the sample was allowed to dry for another hour.

The experimental process of Mott-Schottky was as follows: 10 μL of CuS aqueous solution (0.5 mg mL−1) was added to the polished glassy carbon (GC) electrode by drops and vacuum dried at room temperature for 1 h. The GC electrode was placed in 0.25 M Na2SO4 solution for detection at the frequency of 1 kHz. The photocurrent response test was measured using xenon lamps (HX-F/UV 300) as the excitation light source (200–2500 nm) with an applied voltage of 0.2 V, and the photocurrent signal was detected in PBS buffer solution. EIS was performed in a KCl solution (0.1 M) containing K3Fe(CN)6 and K4Fe(CN)6 (10 mM) with a frequency range of 0.0001 Hz–10 kHz, when the amplitude was set at 5 mV and the potential was 0.2 V.

10 μL of 20 mM EDC solution (dissolved in MES buffer at pH 6.00) and 20 μL of 10 mM NHS (dissolved in PBS buffer at pH 7.40) solution were dropped onto the TMCA modified ITO electrode and incubated at room temperature for 1 h to activate carboxyl groups on the surface of the composite material. Subsequently, the working electrode was carefully washed three times with PBS buffer. Then 20 μL of cDNA solution (4 μM) was dripped onto the working electrode, incubated at 4 °C for 12 h, and then washed with PBS solution several times. 30 μL of 0.5 % BSA solution (PBS buffer, 10 mM, pH 7.40) was added and reacted for 1 h and then washed with PBS solution. Finally, 20 μL of Apt-CuS (C Apt = 8 μM) was added to the working electrode, incubated at 37 °C for 1 h, and then washed with PBS buffer several times. The PEC biosensor with the TMCA-cDNA-Apt-CuS sandwich structure was constructed. To detect the target, drops of various concentrations of AβO solution were added to the working electrode and incubated at 37 °C for 45 min. The working electrode was then carefully cleaned with PBS buffer several times. The photocurrent was tested in the PBS solution. All the error bars in the graph were derived from the standard deviation of the results of three parallel measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TMCA and detection of AβO
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 A describes the synthesis process of the multimodified TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)@CDs@AgNPs composite (TMCA). MIL-101(Cr) was prepared by the hydrothermal method, and then TiO2 was placed in the internal pores of MIL-101(Cr) to obtain TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr). On this basis, using citric acid as a carbon source, CDs in situ grew on the surface of TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr). Then, AgNPs further modified the composite by impregnation method to obtain a multimodified MIL-101(Cr) composite. As shown in Scheme 1B, TMCA was added to the conductive surface of the ITO electrode to make a working electrode. The carboxyl group on the surface of TMCA was activated by EDC/NHS, and cDNA was fixed on the surface of TMCA via an amide bond. After BSA was used to block nonspecific binding sites, Apt-CuS was added to the working electrode. As a result of the complementary nucleic acid sequences of cDNA and Apt, the TMCA-cDNA-Apt-CuS sandwich structure was formed in the PEC sensing system. The transfer process of the photogenerated electron is shown in Scheme 1C. Light irradiation induced the generation of photogenerated electrons in TMCA, which then flowed through the ITO to complete the anode photocurrent loop. In this process, CDs promoted light absorption and improved the photocurrent. The LSPR effect of AgNPs also promoted the absorption of visible light. Furthermore, the high conductivity of AgNPs enhanced electron transfer at the interface and allowed photogenerated electrons to directly transfer to the conduction band of the composite, thereby significantly boosting the photocurrent. In the sandwich detection system, as a p-type semiconductor, CuS captured the photogenerated electrons, resulting in the decline of the photocurrent. When AβO was introduced, the Apt-CuS complex was separated from cDNA due to the stronger affinity between AβO and Apt, leading to destruction of the designed sandwich structure. Consequently, CuS no longer captured the photogenerated electrons, and the photocurrent was restored. This "on-off-on" detection strategy enabled the accurate detection of AβO.

The content of AgNPs in TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)@CDs@AgNPs was optimized, and the results are shown in Fig. 1 A. When the mass fraction of AgNPs was 5 %, the photocurrent density (J) reached the maximum value. The results of the morphological characterization of MIL-101(Cr) and TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr) are shown in Fig. S2. It can be found that there are only a few small fractions of TiO2 observed on the surface of MIL-101(Cr) (Fig. S2B). TiO2 NPs can't be directly observed because they grew in the internal pores of MOFs. The element mapping and N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms shown in the previous work also demonstrated that TiO2 NPs were formed in the pores of MIL-101(Cr) [34]. The previous results showed that MIL-101(Cr) had two pore sizes of 0.8–1.9 nm and 3.0–3.4 nm and TiO2 NPs prepared in MIL-101(Cr) led to a significant decrease in the pore volume. As shown in Fig. 1B, AgNPs were distributed on the surface of the composite. The obvious lattice fringes were observed in the HRTEM image (Fig. 1C). The lattice spacing of 0.230 nm belonged to the (100) plane of CDs [35], and that of 0.236 nm corresponded to the (111) plane of AgNPs [36]. The XRD patterns also confirmed the formation of AgNPs (Fig. S2C). Fig. 1D indicates that the XRD pattern of MIL-101(Cr) matched the simulated pattern for MIL-101(Cr). The diffraction peaks of 2θ = 8.4° and 9.1° in MIL-101(Cr) and TMCA corresponded to the (822) and (911) plane of MIL-101(Cr) based on the simulated XRD, respectively [37]. The broad strong peak in TMCA about 25° was attributed to TiO2 and CDs. Fig. 1D demonstrates that the multimodification kept the crystalline structure of MIL-101(Cr). The XPS spectra of TMCA, as exhibited in Fig. S3, offer a profound insight into its chemical composition. The spectra revealed the presence of distinct elements including C, O, N, Cr, Ti, and Ag. These elements were attributed to the various components of the system, specifically TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr), CDs and AgNPs. The results indicate that TMCA was successfully prepared.

As shown in Fig. 1E, when CDs were modified in TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr), the light absorption of the composite was significantly improved in the range of 330–410 nm. When AgNPs were further modified on TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr)@CDs, the LSPR effect of AgNPs promoted the absorption of the composite in the visible region, which was conducive to improving the photocurrent. Based on Fig. 1F, it can be seen that through continuous modification of CDs and AgNPs on TiO2-in-MIL-101(Cr), J increased from 0.285 μA cm−2 (MIL-101(Cr)) to 5.47 μA cm−2 (TMCA) by an approximate factor of 19. This demonstrates that it is feasible to improve the PEC response of MOFs composites via the modification of CDs and AgNPs. Furthermore, we found that continuous modification of CDs and AgNPs improved the transport efficiency of the photogenerated carriers of the composites (Fig. S4).

Fig. S5A shows clear crystal fringes of CuS, indicating that CuS had a good crystal form and d = 0.301 nm belonged to the crystal plane (102) of CuS [38]. The slope of the tangent line at the inflection point of the Mott-Schottky curve was negative (Fig. S5B), indicating that the prepared CuS was a p-type semiconductor [39]. The flat band potential of CuS was directly read as 0.01 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) through the intersection of the tangent line and the horizontal coordinate. As shown in Fig. S5C, Apt had strong absorption at 280 nm. Pure CuS exhibited weak absorption in the range of 250–290 nm, while CuS-Apt showed a significant increase in absorption in this range after the modification of Apt, indicating a successful binding of Apt to CuS. In addition, the zeta potential of CuS became more negative after modification of Apt, which was due to the negative charge of Apt (Fig. S5D). The characterization results above declare that Apt-CuS was successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and condition optimization of the PEC biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The step-up modification of the PEC interface was studied using EIS. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the electron transfer resistance (R et) of TMCA was relatively small (curve a). After modification of cDNA, R et increased due to electrostatic repulsion of negatively charged cDNA against [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- (curves b). As BSA has poor conductivity, it led to a subsequent increase in R et (curves c). After incubation of Apt-CuS on the TMCA-cDNA electrode, R et increased further (curve d) due to the larger steric hindrance effect caused by the addition of Apt. When TMCA-cDNA-Apt-CuS bound with AβO, the stronger affinity between Apt and AβO resulted in the detachment of Apt-CuS from the electrode, resulting in a weakening of the steric hindrance effect of Apt and a decrease of R et (curve e). The photocurrent response results are shown in Fig. 2B. Under irradiation, due to the enhanced light absorption of CDs and the LSPR effect of AgNPs, the working electrode of TMCA had a strong J of approximately 5.50 μA cm−2 (curve a). Due to the steric hindrance effect of DNA molecules and the blocking of BSA, which prevented the photogenerated electron diffusion, J gradually decreased to 4.76 μA cm−2 (curve b) and 4.37 μA cm−2 (curve c). After the combination of TMCA-cDNA and Apt-CuS through base complementary pairing, J was reduced to 2.86 μA cm−2 due to the capture of photogenerated electrons by the p-type semiconductor CuS (curve d). In the presence of the target AβO, J recovered significantly (curve e). This is due to the preferential binding of Apt-CuS to AβO, resulting in the detachment of Apt-CuS from the cDNA and the disruption of the sandwich detection system. This led to a decrease in the capture of photogenerated electrons by CuS and thus to a recovery of J. The above results prove that the PEC biosensor with specific recognition of AβO had been successfully constructed.

To intuitively understand the role of the p-type semiconductor CuS in the PEC biosensor, a working electrode without CuS (TMCA-cDNA-Apt) was assembled to detect different concentrations of AβO, and the results are shown in Fig. 2C. After the addition of Apt, J had little change, only decreasing to 3.92 μA cm−2 (curve b). Although the addition of different concentrations of AβO can restore the PEC signal, the change was not obvious (curve c-g). In the absence of CuS as a signal amplifier, the PEC biosensor had a small detection range. Even with the addition of high concentration AβO (0.5 μM), J changed only by 0.42 μA cm−2 (from 3.92 μA cm−2 to 4.34 μA cm−2), showing a low sensitivity. This finding suggests that the introduction of p-type semiconductor CuS can improve the sensitivity of the detection method and widen the detection range.

After incubating with cDNA, the working electrode was dried at room temperature, and then the products loaded on the surface of the working electrode were gently scraped and dissolved in ultrapure water to measure the UV–visible absorption spectrum. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when the incubation time reached 12 h, the absorption at 280 nm increased significantly. Since continued extension of the incubation time may lead to cDNA hydrolysis, the cDNA incubation time was selected as 12 h. When the incubation temperature of cDNA was 4 °C, the absorption value at 280 nm was the maximum (Fig. 3B), indicating that the binding effect was the best. However, we found that absorption decreased when the incubation temperature increased. It is speculated that higher temperature may compromise the stability of cDNA and weaken the binding between cDNA and MOFs composites, thereby reducing the absorption at 280 nm. As shown in Fig. 3C, the optimal concentration of cDNA was 4 μM, and the J was almost unchanged when it was greater than 4 μM. The concentration of Apt-CuS incubated with the working electrode was also optimized. When the Apt-CuS concentration reached 8 μM, J was the smallest (Fig. 3D). Furthermore, the value of J abnormally increased when the concentration of Apt-CuS reached 16 μM. This increase can be attributed to the possibility that an excessive concentration may have caused Apt aggregation, subsequently impairing its ability to bind effectively with cDNA.

PH is an important factor that cannot be ignored for the detection of biological samples. As shown in Fig. 3E, the amount of photocurrent recovery varied with the change in pH. When the pH was 7.00, the change in J reached maximum. Therefore, the optimal detection pH was 7.00. Fig. 3F suggests that upon reaching an incubation time of 45 min, J attained its peak value. Subsequently, further extension of the incubation time failed to elicit a significant enhancement in J. These results suggest that AβO had undergone complete reaction for 45 min. Fig. 3G shows that the optimal incubation temperature of AβO was 37 °C.

The relationship between J and AβO concentration was studied under optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 A, J exhibited an increase with a concomitant increase in AβO concentration. Furthermore, in the concentration range of 5 fM to 1 μM, J had a linear relationship with the logarithm of AβO concentration (Fig. 4B). The linear equation was as follows: J (μA∙cm−2) = 0.1548 logC AβO (pM) + 3.268 (R 2 = 0.9963, S/N = 3), and the detection limit (LOD) was 4.36 fM. Compared to other detection methods outlined in Table S1, the developed TMCA based PEC biosensor using multisignal amplification technology exhibited a wide detection range coupled with a heightened sensitivity. AβM, AβF, tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), lysozyme (Lys) and insulin (Ins) were selected as interfering substances, and the results are shown in Fig. 4C. Compared to the target AβO (10 pM), the degree of change in J caused by the disruptor was significantly lower at 100 pM. The results demonstrate that the constructed PEC biosensor had good selectivity. We evaluated the storage stability and reproducibility of the PEC biosensor. After being placed at 4 °C and 25 °C in the dark for 30 days, the value of J of the PEC biosensor was 90.19 % and 76.14 % of the initial value, respectively, indicating that the PEC biosensor possessed good storage stability at 4 °C (Fig. 4D). Finally, when six identical working electrodes were used to detect AβO, the RSD of J was 4.76 % (Fig. 4E), indicating good reproducibility of the prepared working electrodes.

To verify the applicability of the PEC biosensor for the detection of AβO, the labeled recovery experiment was carried out in normal human serum. Three concentrations of AβO (0.5 μM, 1 nM, 10 fM) were added by the standard addition method. As shown in Table 1 , the apparent recovery factors were 98.12–100.4 % and the RSD was 2.73–3.07 %. The results indicate that the PEC biosensor had certain reliability and good potential for application for the determination of AβO content in human serum.

Based on this work, it is found that although MIL-101(Cr) had weak PEC performance, its PEC performance can be effectively improved through multiple modifications. Through internal (TiO2) and external (CDs) decoration, the construction of sandwich structure of the composite enhanced the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The further modification of metal nanoparticles (AgNPs) improved the absorption of visible light and electron transport. Thus, the PEC performance of the composite was improved. The findings also demonstrated that the introduction of a p-type semiconductor (e.g. CuS) can capture photogenerated electrons and amplify the detection signal, which was profitable for the construction of an "on-off-on" mode PEC biosensor. The PEC biosensor exhibited optimal performance at a pH of 7.00, an incubation temperature of 37 °C, and an incubation time of 45 min when used for the detection of AβO. The results demonstrated that the developed PEC biosensor had good selectivity, storage stability, and reproducibility. This detection method is expected to open up a sensitive, reliable, and economical new method for the early detection and diagnosis of AD.

Jianwei Lin: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Danni Lin: Visualization, Investigation. Shaopeng Wang: Investigation. Qihong Liao: Investigation. Fanhui Meng: Investigation. Jinghua Chen: Conceptualization. Zhizhong Han: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2019J01300 and 2023J011224), Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates (202210392003).
